coredata.dc:title: Co-deposition of carbon dots and reduced graphene oxide nanosheets on carbon-fiber microelectrode surface for selective detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Jian
Xie, Zhigang
Wallace, Gordon
Wang, Xungai
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon dots (CD) decorated graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets were electrochemically reduced and deposited onto carbon fiber (CF) to fabricate microelectrodes for highly sensitive and selective dopamine (DA) detection, in the presence of ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA). The results have shown that surface modification considerably increases the electrocatalytic activity of the carbon fiber microelectrode. Due to possible aggregation of the rGO sheets during deposition, modifying the microelectrode surface with rGO sheets alone cannot achieve the selectivity required for simultaneous detection of DA, AA and UA. Through attaching CD onto GO sheets, the rGO+CD/CF microelectrode performance was significantly improved. The existence of CD on GO sheets can effectively avoid inter-layer stacking of the rGO sheets and provide increased surface area for neurotransmitter-electrode interaction enhancement. The CD can also increase the charge storage capacity of GO sheets. This is the first report on applying both CD and rGO for surface modification of carbon fiber microelectrode. The rGO+CD/CF microelectrode has achieved a linear DA detection concentration range of 0.1–100μM, with a detection limit of 0.02μM. The sensitivity of the microelectrode towards DA was as high as 6.5nA/μM, which is significantly higher than previously reported carbon fiber microelectrodes. The highly sensitive all-carbon based microelectrodes should find use in a number of biomedical applications, such as neurotransmitter detection, neural signal recording and cell physiology studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon fiber
Carbon dot
Graphene oxide
Dopamine
Microelectrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical detection of DA, AA and UA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) diameter distribution of the prepared CD; (c) UV absorption (Abs), fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of aqueous CD solution (inset: pictures of the CD aqueous solution before and after 365nm UV radiation); (d) Fluorescence (FL) emission spectra of the carbon dot solution at different excitation wavelengths (from bottom to top, excitation wavelengths are 405nm, 305nm, 395nm, 315nm, 385nm, 335nm, 375nm, 365nm, 355nm and 345nm).
(a) XRD curve and (b) FTIR spectrum of the CD; TEM images of (c) graphene oxide sheet and (d) graphene oxide sheet attached with carbon dots.
SEM image of (a) pure carbon fiber, (b) the carbon fiber with electrochemically deposited GO and (c) the carbon fiber with electrochemically deposited GO and CD.

(a) XPS profiles of the CF, CD, GO, rGO/CF and rGO+CD/CF samples; High resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of the (b) CF, (c) GO, (d) CD, (e) rGO/CF and (f) rGO+CD/CF samples; (g) Raman spectra of the CF, GO, rGO/CF and rGO+CD/CF samples.

CV responses of (a) 5mMK3Fe(CN)6 in 0.5M KCl solution (scan rate: 50mV/s), (b) 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.0) containing 100μM DA, 1mM AA and 100μM UA (scan rate: 100mV/s) on three different carbon fiber microelectrodes and (c) 0.1M pure PBS solution (pH 7.0) on the rGO+CD/CF microelectrode.

(a) CV responses at different scan rates of 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.0) containing 100μM DA, 1mM AA and 100μM UA on the rGO+CD/CF microelectrodes; (b) DPV responses of a 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.0) containing 200μM AA, 25μM UA and different concentrations of DA on the rGO+CD/CF microelectrodes; (c) Linear fit of the DPV peak current to DA concentration; (d) stability test of a rGO+CD/CF microelectrode against the number of DA (100μM) detection cycles.

head.ce:title: Co-deposition of carbon dots and reduced graphene oxide nanosheets on carbon-fiber microelectrode surface for selective detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ARC Centre of Excellence for Electromaterials Science (ACES), Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: ARC Centre of Excellence for Electromaterials Science (ACES)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Deakin University, Institute for Frontier Materials, Geelong, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Intelligent Polymer Research Institute (IPRI), University of Wollongong, NSW 2522, Australia

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute for Frontier Materials, GTP Research Deakin University Geelong Waurn Ponds Campus, Locked Bag 20000, Geelong, VIC 3220, Australia. Tel.: +61 3 52272704.
Corresponding authors at: Deakin University, Institute for Frontier Materials, Geelong, Australia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon dots (CD) decorated graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets were electrochemically reduced and deposited onto carbon fiber (CF) to fabricate microelectrodes for highly sensitive and selective dopamine (DA) detection, in the presence of ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA). The results have shown that surface modification considerably increases the electrocatalytic activity of the carbon fiber microelectrode. Due to possible aggregation of the rGO sheets during deposition, modifying the microelectrode surface with rGO sheets alone cannot achieve the selectivity required for simultaneous detection of DA, AA and UA. Through attaching CD onto GO sheets, the rGO+CD/CF microelectrode performance was significantly improved. The existence of CD on GO sheets can effectively avoid inter-layer stacking of the rGO sheets and provide increased surface area for neurotransmitter-electrode interaction enhancement. The CD can also increase the charge storage capacity of GO sheets. This is the first report on applying both CD and rGO for surface modification of carbon fiber microelectrode. The rGO+CD/CF microelectrode has achieved a linear DA detection concentration range of 0.1–100μM, with a detection limit of 0.02μM. The sensitivity of the microelectrode towards DA was as high as 6.5nA/μM, which is significantly higher than previously reported carbon fiber microelectrodes. The highly sensitive all-carbon based microelectrodes should find use in a number of biomedical applications, such as neurotransmitter detection, neural signal recording and cell physiology studies.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is one of the most important neurotransmitters in the human brain, affecting renal, cardiovascular, central nervous, and hormonal systems [1–4]. Dopamine is critical to our attention, learning and memory [5]. It is also believed that abnormalities of dopamine concentration are linked to many neurological disorders; Parkinson’s, Huntinton’s, schizophrenia and epilepsy [6–8]. It is therefore of great importance to develop an accurate, sensitive, fast and low cost technique for monitoring DA level. Techniques used for DA detection include gas/liquid chromatography [9], mass/raman spectroscopy [10], fluorescence detection [11], colorimetric detection [12] and electrochemical detection [13]. Among them, the electrochemical approach has least instrumentation requirements and the most convenient sample preparation method. Therefore, it has attracted enormous research interest resulting in the development of electrochemical dopamine sensors [14].

The ideal dopamine sensor should have three basic characteristics. First, it should have high sensitivity. The DA concentration in living systems can be very low (nanomolar (nM) level), therefore the electrode should be able to examine ultra-low intensity oxidation signals. Second, DA sensor needs high selectivity. In natural biological environment, DA always co-exists with uric acid (UA) and ascorbic acid (AA), and they have oxidation potentials close to DA. In this regard, a practically applicable DA sensor should selectively magnify the DA oxidation signal and reduce or shift the signals of the UA and AA, interfering molecules. Third, a diagnostic dopamine sensor should be able to work with an extremely small sample volume, so a reduced electrode size is essential. Microelectrodes use micrometer-sized conductive wires or fibers as the sensing media, and they have shown outstanding performance in neural signal recording, neurotransmitter sensing and neural stimulation [15,16]. Compared with their macro-sized counterparts, microelectrodes normally exhibit improved signal-to-noise ratio, fast response time and can work with limited sample volumes.

Among many functional materials that have been used as dopamine electrodes, carbon materials (e.g., activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, carbon fibers, graphene/graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets and carbon dots) have been shown to excel in terms of biocompatibility, sensitivity, selectivity, stability and cost [17–21]. Graphene and chemically reduced graphene oxide have been extensively investigated in biosensing [22–24]. Their high surface area and versatile surface functionality can lead to high sensitivity and selectivity. It has been found that a porous coating of reduced graphene oxide on a gold substrate electrode significantly increased sensitivity towards DA detection, and also lowered the oxidation potential of AA to enable selective detection of DA [25,26]. However, due to their two-dimensional structure, the strong van der Waals and π − π stacking interactions among graphene or reduced graphene oxide sheets can easily result in re-stacking, causing reduced specific surface area and electrochemical sensitivity [27,28].

Carbon fibers are manufactured from polymeric precursor fibers through carbonization, and the high carbon content (>95%) makes carbon fibers electrically conductive, corrosion resistant, biologically safe and inert [29]. Therefore, carbon fiber has been considered as an ideal candidate for making bioelectrodes [30], especially microelectrodes [19,31], for different in vitro and in vivo applications. Pure carbon fibers have a low specific surface area, therefore surface modification and functionization are critical to achieve ideal sensing performance. For example, chemically reduced graphene sheets were dip-coated onto single carbon fiber surface for DA detection [30]. The modified microelectrode exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity, also with demonstrated in vivo application potential. In another research [32], direct electrochemical deposition was used to reduce GO sheets to graphene and coated onto carbon fiber surface. The resulted microelectrodes achieved much improved sensitivity, but with limited working range of DA concentration. Carbon dots (CD) are non-toxic materials with versatile surface chemistries. They have been investigated in many biomedical applications, especially biosensing and bioimaging [33]. Previous research [34] has revealed that by incorporating CD with metal oxides, the nanocomposite could obtain considerably improved charge storage capacity.

In this work, three kinds of microelectrodes were fabricated: pure carbon fibers, carbon fibers with electrochemically deposited graphene oxide sheets and carbon fibers with electrochemically deposited graphene oxide sheets modified with carbon dots. The surface morphology and chemistry of the microelectrodes were characterized using different techniques. By examining the electrochemical properties of the microelectrodes using cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry, it has been demonstrated that surface modification on carbon fiber microelectrode using reduced graphene oxide and carbon dots can achieve a high sensitivity and selectivity in dopamine detection, in the presence of ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA).

Graphite flakes, sodium nitrate (NaNO3, ≥99.0%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4, ≥99.0%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30wt% in H2O), sodium citrate dihydrate (C6H5O7·2H2O·3Na, ≥99.0%), ammonium bicarbonate (NH4HCO3, ≥99.0%), lithium perchlorate (LiClO4, battery grade), dopamine hydrochloride, L-Ascorbic acid, uric acid, potassium phosphate dibasic (K2HPO4) and potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Australia), and they were used as received. Unsized carbon fibers without any surface treatment were used in this work.

GO was prepared by the oxidation of graphite flasks according to Hummer’s method [35]. Briefly, 10g of graphite flake and 7.5g of NaNO3 were first mixed with 300mL of 98% H2SO4 before 40g of KMnO4 was added in. The mixture was stirred for 3days at room temperature and poured into 1L of 1% H2O2 to remove any unreacted chemicals. The mixture was filtered and washed with deionized water. The resulting GO was re-dispersed in de-ionized water and purified by dialysis for one week. The final brown suspension was dried at 40°C under vacuum before use.

Carbon quantum dots with strong blue fluorescence were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal process. Briefly, 0.2g of C6H5O7·2H2O·3Na and 1.5g of NH4HCO3 were dissolved in 10mL of de-ionized water to form a clear solution. The solution was then transferred to a 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment. After heating at 180°C in oven for 4h and cooling down to room temperature, a light yellow solution was obtained. The solution was dialyzed in a cellulose ester dialysis tube (MW: 100–500Da) for 2days to purify the CD. Dry CD powders were finally obtained after freeze-drying.

Glass capillary tubes with an inner diameter of 1.2mm were used to fabricate microelectrodes. After ultrasonic cleaning in ethanol and acetone, a single carbon fiber was first loaded into the capillary tube with the aid of vacuum suction, and then the loaded tube was manually pulled into two microelectrodes by heating the middle part of the tube with a gas torch. As shown the microelectrode structure in Fig. 1 , one end of the carbon fiber was sealed in the pulled tip while the other end was connected to a gold wire through silver epoxy. The protrusion of the carbon fiber at the microelectrode tip was maintained at around 2mm.

Microelectrodes containing three kinds of carbon fibers were fabricated in this work: pure carbon fibers (CF), carbon fibers with electrochemically reduced GO on the surface (rGO/CF) and carbon fibers modified with electrochemically reduced GO and CD (rGO+CD/CF).

Electrochemical reduction process was used to deposit rGO and rGO+CD sheets on the carbon fiber surface for preparing the rGO/CF and rGO+CD/CF microelectrodes. For the rGO/CF microelectrodes, a 4mg/mL GO dispersion containing 0.2M LiClO4 was used as the electrolyte. A three-electrode system was used with the CF microelectrode as the working electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode and a platinum wire as the counter electrode. Electrochemical deposition was carried out on an electrochemistry working station (e-Corder 401) using a cyclic voltammetry (CV) scanning from −1.2V to 0.6V at a scan rate of 100mV/s for 30 cycles. In preparing the rGO+CD/CF microelectrode, 50μg/mL CD were added into the same GO dispersion. Before carrying out the electrochemical deposition at the same conditions, the mixture was kept static for 1h to achieve effective attachment of CD onto GO sheets after stirring for 15min.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a JEOL 2100F Field Emission transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The CD diameter was analyzed on the TEM image using Image J 1.48 software. Surface morphology of the carbon fibers was observed on a Zeiss Supra 55VP scanning electron microscope (SEM) without any gold coating. The optical absorption spectra of the samples were obtained using a Varian Cary 3E ultraviolet visible (UV−vis) spectrophotometer. The excitation and emission spectra of the CD were acquired on an Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer. XPS data were acquired using a Kratos AXIS Ultra (DLD) spectrometer equipped with a monochromated Al Kα X-ray source (energy ∼1486.6eV). Survey spectra were recorded at 1eV/step and a pass energy of 160eV while high-resolution spectra were performed at 0.1eV/step and a pass energy of 20eV. The pressure in the analysis chamber was maintained at about 3×10−9 Torr. Raman measurements were performed on a Renishaw InVia Raman microscope equipped with a 514nm laser. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a Bruker Optics spectroscopy in attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Panalytical X-ray diffractometer using Cu radiation of 1.54Å. The samples were scanned in the 2θ range of 10°–50° with a step size of 0.05°.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of DA, AA and UA
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical detection of DA was conducted in a 0.1M PBS solution (pH 7.0, prepared using K2HPO4 and KH2PO4 in Milli-Q water) containing 100μM DA, 1mM AA and 100μM UA. By using three kinds of microelectrodes as the working electrode in the three electrode system, the CV scanning from −0.2 to 0.8V was used during the detection. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) scanning was used to examine the DA detection of the rGO+CD/CF microelectrode at different DA concentrations.

Fig. 2 a shows the TEM image of the carbon dots synthesized using the hydrothermal process, and they had uniform size with a diameter of 4.96±0.65nm (Fig. 2b). The UV absorption, fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of the CD in an aqueous solution are shown in Fig. 2c. It can be seen that the positions of UV absorption maximum peak (332nm) and fluorescent excitation peak (345nm) are very close. The inset image clearly shows the bright blue light displayed from the CD solution under UV light with excitation at 365nm, which corresponds to the emission spectra. It has also been observed that the CD exhibited the highest fluorescent intensity when they were excited by 345nm UV light (Fig. 2d).

The broad diffraction peak in the XRD result shown in Fig. 3 a confirms the amorphous nature of the CD. FTIR spectrum (Fig. 3b) reveals many spectroscopic characteristics of the CD, including OH absorption bands at 3400cm−1, NH vibration at 3200cm−1, CH stretching vibrations at 3020cm−1 and 2840cm−1, CC vibration at 1580cm−1, COO absorption at 1420cm−1, NO stretching vibration at 1360cm−1 and COC vibration at 1200cm−1 [36–38].

Chemically synthesized graphene oxide nanosheets contain many functional groups (e.g., OH, CO2H, O), therefore hydrogen bonding involving GO sheets or between GO sheets and other nanomaterials is possible [39,40]. Through this covalent bonding approach, CD were attached onto the GO sheets (Fig. 3c) without any obvious CD agglomeration, as shown in Fig. 3d.

Fig. 4 a shows the surface morphology of the pure carbon fiber used in this study. The pure carbon fibers have an average diameter of 7μm. They also exhibit a grooved surface structure which is typical for the carbon fibers produced using wet-spun polyacrylonitrile (PAN) as the precursor fiber. Using cyclic voltammetry, GO sheets were electrochemically reduced and deposited onto the carbon fiber surface, as shown in Fig. 4b. It can be seen that the surface grooves on the carbon fiber were completely covered by rGO sheets and the coated carbon fiber still exhibited a uniform morphology with the diameter of 9.2±0.3μm. Compared with applying chemically reduced graphene sheets onto carbon fibers using a dip coating method [41], electrochemical deposition is a simpler single-step approach enabling simultaneous reduction and deposition, which also results in a more uniform layer. When CD were attached onto the GO sheets and co-deposited onto the carbon fiber surface using the same electrochemical process, the resulting carbon fiber had a diameter of 9.8±0.4μm (Fig. 4c). It can be found that the existence of the CD didn’t significantly change the surface morphology of the coated carbon fiber, and the slight increase in fiber diameter was attributed to the increased inter-layer distance of the GO sheets with CD attachment.

From the high resolution C1s XPS curve fitting in Fig. 5b, it was found that the CF curve has four peaks with binding energies assigned at 290.7, 287.4, 285.2 and 284.5eV, which are attributed to π-π*, CO, sp3 C and sp2 C, respectively [42,43]. Meanwhile, the GO and CD curves also have four fitted peaks with similar binding energies, suggesting the existence of OCO (or CN for the CD sample), CO, CO and CC (Fig. 5c & d). After electrochemical deposition of the GO sheets onto carbon fiber, the rGO/CF sample had a C1s XPS spectrum consisting of four peaks: CO (288.9eV), COH (285.7eV), sp3 C (285.2eV) and sp2 C (284.5eV) (Fig. 3e), which are typical for electrochemically reduced graphene oxide [44]. Fig. 3f shows the C1s XPS fitting of the rGO+CD/CF sample, which exhibits the same characteristic peaks as the rGO/CF sample.

Raman spectroscopy is a useful tool to investigate chemical structure of carbon materials [45]. It can be found in Fig. 3g that the Raman spectrum of all four samples shows the characteristic D band at 1350cm−1 and G band at 1595cm−1 (weak Raman signal was obtained on the CD sample due to possible strong background signal). The intensity of the Raman spectrum of the CF sample was much lower than that of the other three samples, which should be resulted from the highly dense structure and low specific surface area of carbon fibers. By calculating the intensity ratio (ID/IG ) of D band and G band, both CF and GO samples had a low ID/IG ratio (0.92 and 0.89, respectively), which reveals the low density of defects in their structure [44]. Electrochemical reduction converts many oxygenated groups on GO sheets to non-oxygenated groups and generates different topological defects and vacancies [46], therefore both rGO/CF and rGO+CD/CF samples had a higher ID/IG ratio (1.23 and 1.15) than pure carbon fiber.

Cyclic voltammetry using a 5mMK3Fe(CN)6 aqueous solution containing 0.5M KCl was used to probe the electrochemical properties (Fig. 6 a). A clear increase in the CV current can be seen after depositing electrochemically reduced GO sheets on the carbon fiber, suggesting an increased electrode area. By incorporating CD onto the GO sheets, there was a further increase in the current.

Cyclic voltammetry was then carried out using the three microelectrodes under investigation in a 100μM DA PBS solution containing 1mM AA and 100μM UA (Fig. 6b). Using a pure carbon fiber working electrode, none of the three neurotransmitters in the solution were detected. After GO sheets were electrochemically deposited onto the carbon fiber, an obvious oxidation peak at 0.20V was observed using the rGO/CF microelectrode. However the individual oxidation peak of the three neurotransmitters were not observed. After decorating the GO sheets with CD, the oxidation peaks of all three neurotransmitters in the solution were clearly obvious in the cyclic voltammograms obtained (with background response shown in Fig. 6c). It can be believed that this improvement has two reasons. Firstly, the existence of the carbon dots functions as the physical barrier to prevent complete restacking of the reduced graphene oxide nanosheets, which helps to maintain a high area of accessible sheet surface for dopamine interaction, without losing any surface functions of the nanosheets. Secondly, carbon dots themselves can interact with dopamine molecules to increase charge storage capability of the electrodes for signal amplification.

The electrochemical oxidation kinetics of the rGO+CD/CF microelectrode was examined at different CV scan rates ranging from 25mV/s to 200mV/s (Fig. 7 a). Results show that the current of all three oxidation peaks increased with scan rate, and the peak potential was slightly shifted to more positive positions. The peak current of DA was found to be proportional to the square root of the scan rate (lower than 100mV/s). Suggesting a diffusion controlled process however, a linear relationship between the DA peak current and the scan rate was found at higher scan rates, suggesting a surface absorption-controlled process [47].

The relationship between the oxidation peak current and DA concentration has been examined using the more sensitive electrochemical differential pulse voltammetry. As shown in Fig. 7b, the peak current increased with increasing DA concentration from 0.02μM to 150μM. A linear relationship has been found with the DA concentration range of 0.1–100μM, with a detection limit of 0.02μM (Fig. 7c). The linear regression equation can be expressed as Ipa (μA)=0.0065C (μM) +0.0425 (R =0.9963), therefore the detection sensitivity is obtained as 6.5nA/μM, which is much higher than that of other graphene treated carbon fiber microelectrodes [32,41].

In addition, the stability of the rGO+CD/CF microelectrode was carefully examined as well. As shown in Fig. 7d, the variation of oxidation peak potential during 10 repeated DA detections was negligible, meanwhile the peak current intensity retained around 90% of its initial value. The promising stability results have indicated the feasibility of surface modification approach used in this study.

In summary, carbon fiber microelectrodes were surface modified with electrochemically reduced GO sheets and GO+CD composite sheets. Surface modification has considerably increased the electrode surface area and electrocatalytic activity. When these three kinds of microelectrodes were used to simultaneously detect DA, AA and UA, the rGO/CF electrode exhibited only a broad oxidation peak covering the oxidation behavior of all three neurotransmitters. Because of the enhancement of charge storage capacity and neurotransmitter-electrode interaction, the rGO+CD/CF microelectrode could detect all three neurotransmitters with high sensitivity and selectivity. The DPV scanning has revealed that the GO and CD modified microelectrode had a linear DA detection concentration range of 0.1–100μM, with a detection limit of 0.02μM. The results suggest the effectiveness of using carbon dots to enhance biosensing performance of reduced graphene nanosheets, presumable with two reasons: physical barrier effect to avoid inter-layer stacking of graphene nanosheets and signal amplification function from increased charge storage ability. The highly sensitive microelectrodes developed in this research provide promising application potential in various in vitro and in vivo biomedical practices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially support by Australian Research Council (ARC) through ARC Centre of Excellence for Electromaterials Science (ACES). The present work was carried out with the support of the Deakin Advanced Characterisation Facility. The authors also would like to thank the Carbon Nexus facility at Deakin University for kindly providing unsized carbon fibers.
